summary:
The Time of Troubles (Smutnoye Vremya, 1598–1613 AD) was a catastrophic period of political crisis, social upheaval, famine, and foreign invasion in Russia that followed the extinction of the Rurik dynasty and nearly resulted in the permanent dismemberment of the Russian state. When Tsar Fyodor I (the last Rurik) died childless in 1598, Boris Godunov — his capable but ruthless regent — seized power. His reign (1598–1605) was undermined by the Great Famine of 1601–1603 (possibly caused by a volcanic winter from the 1600 eruption of Huaynaputina in Peru — one of history's longest-range disaster chains), which killed perhaps 2 million Russians, destroyed Godunov's political legitimacy, and created a population of displaced, desperate people susceptible to manipulation.

The crisis deepened through a cascade of pretenders claiming to be the murdered son of [[event:2410|Ivan the Terrible]], Tsarevich Dmitry: the First False Dmitry (1605–1606), a Polish-backed pretender who briefly took Moscow before being overthrown; the Second False Dmitry (the 'Brigand of Tushino'); and a Polish invasion that actually occupied Moscow (1610–1612) while a Swedish force occupied Novgorod. Russia's survival required the formation of a national militia by Kuzma Minin (a butcher) and Prince Dmitry Pozharsky, who expelled the Poles from Moscow in 1612. The Zemsky Sobor (national assembly) of 1613 elected the 16-year-old Mikhail Romanov as Tsar, founding the [[event:2412|Romanov dynasty]] that would rule until 1917. The Time of Troubles profoundly traumatized Russian political culture, reinforcing the conviction that strong autocratic central power was essential to prevent the chaos of competing claims — a lesson that shaped Russian political thought for centuries.
